topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The folder was tucked deep inside a dusty textbook, a relic from freshman year. I’d been clearing out my old binders, a mindless task, when I found it: my personal statement, perfectly formatted, sitting next to a printed example titled "Illustrating Intellectual Curiosity." My hands started to sweat instantly. I picked it up, the paper feeling strangely slick under my fingertips. The prose was too polished, the anecdote too perfectly crafted. Every sentence felt like a carefully placed brick in a structure I wasn't supposed to be building. I spent the next hour staring at the words, my breath catching in shallow gasps. I checked the room repeatedly, listening for sounds that weren't there—a faint click from the hallway, the hum of the refrigerator seeming suddenly menacing. I slid the essay back into the binder, the plastic cover feeling flimsy, and slammed the drawer shut with unnecessary force. I kept glancing over my shoulder, convinced someone was watching me move, cataloging the exact trajectory of my every breath.

[story 2]

It was an anomaly. A misplaced file on a shared drive, and there it was—my essay, transformed into some aspirational masterpiece. The air in my small apartment suddenly felt thin, heavy, like breathing water. I couldn't look away. The structure was flawless, the voice imbued with a confidence I didn't possess. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, radiating outward. I started pacing the length of my living room, my movements jerky and quick. I kept touching my neck, feeling a strange vibration beneath my skin. Every shadow seemed to shift, elongating into something indistinct and watchful. I tried to rationalize it—a prank, a clerical error—but the silence of the room amplified the frantic thumping in my chest. I ran to the window, peering down at the street, convinced that the mundane sounds of traffic were actually coded messages, and that the person who left the essay was waiting just beyond the glass.

[story 3]

I found the document tucked into the back corner of my laptop’s downloads folder. It was my story, rewritten, elevated to some standard of perfection I couldn't meet. A tremor started in my hands, a subtle, involuntary shaking that I tried to mask by gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles were white. I began to scrutinize the metadata, clicking through timestamps and file paths with obsessive speed. Each click felt loud in the quiet room. My focus narrowed until the rest of the world dissolved; there was only the screen, the stark, eloquent paragraphs, and the relentless feeling that I was being observed from every angle. I stood up abruptly, needing to move, needing distance. As I walked toward the kitchen, I heard a sound—a soft scrape, like something being dragged across linoleum—and I froze, every muscle tensing, waiting for the inevitable confirmation that I was not alone in this unsettling surveillance.
